随着对执法策略越来越严格的审查，警方使用一种类型的逮捕令来整体监控谷歌用户的位置，这是它面临的第一个法律和政治挑战。
弗吉尼亚州和旧金山的刑事被告正在对“GeoFence”搜查令提出异议，当局可以使用这种搜查令，通过科技公司存储的用户位置历史，扫描地理区域和时间段，寻找嫌疑人。随着这些动议最快在8月份等待辩论和可能的决定，纽约的立法者正在推动立法禁止这种做法。
警方在这两起案件中都求助于Alphabet Google，该公司表示，从2017年到2018年，这类请求的数量增长了1500%，从2018年到2019年，又增长了500%。这家搜索和广告巨头创建了一个特殊的程序来履行逮捕令，但也提出了对执法部门的要求侵犯隐私的担忧。
隐私权倡导者担心，如果更多健身跟踪器、拼车应用和其他公司开始存储用户的精细位置历史，以完善他们的产品和服务，谷歌的合规可能会导致当局从这些公司寻求类似的数据。
“如果我们不取缔这种做法，我们将看到这个不断扩大的地理定位搜查令世界，它将追踪大量的人，”非营利性倡导组织监视技术监督项目(SurveMonitoring Technology Supervisor Project)的执行主任阿尔伯特·福克斯·卡恩(Albert Fox Cahn)说。
巧合的是，在5月份乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)在明尼阿波利斯警方拘留期间被杀后，执法部门受到了批评。
纽约州民主党参议员路易斯·塞普尔韦达(Luis Sepúlveda)是州参议院犯罪受害者、犯罪和矫正委员会主席，他在4月份共同发起了一项法案，宣布逮捕令和非正式要求提供此类数据为非法。塞普尔韦达警告说，与截停搜身等许多其他警察战术一样，在有色人种的社区中，会不成比例地使用地栅栏搜查令。
旧金山公设辩护律师上个月提交了一项动议，要求撤销2019年2月逮捕30岁的拉坎·道斯(Laquan Dawes)时使用的这样一项逮捕令，该逮捕令与该市日落区的一起入室盗窃案有关。他们辩称，当局在两个多小时的时间里，在房子周围半个街区的范围内搜查谷歌用户，最终将矛头对准了道斯，侵犯了第四修正案对不合理搜查和扣押的权利。
另外，在弗吉尼亚州，辩护律师正在寻求在8月举行听证会，代表25岁的奥卡洛·查特里(Okello Chtrie)提出类似的论点。查特里被控武装抢劫里士满郊外的一家银行。
法庭文件显示，这家科技公司创建了一个分三步走的流程，在满足当局的地理围栏搜查令的同时，尽可能多地保持用户隐私。
谷歌搜索其整个账户数据库，启用位置历史记录，以检查哪些用户通过搜查令指定的一般区域和时间段。该公司将这些信息汇编成匿名数据集，供警方使用。
当局审查哪些设备可能是感兴趣的，可能会要求提供有关特定用户的额外位置数据作为上下文。
提交给美国弗吉尼亚州东区地区法院的文件显示，在警方要求提供有关在一小时内经过犯罪现场150米范围内的设备的信息后，谷歌梳理了每一个选择使用位置历史功能的用户，以缩小潜在匹配的范围。
根据谷歌在该案中提交的文件，截至去年，大约三分之一的谷歌用户选择了加入，将与数千万人甚至可能是数亿人有关的信息发送到一个内部称为Sensorvault的数据库中。谷歌的一名代表拒绝提供确切的数字。
法庭文件显示，该公司的律师没有对查特里一案中的逮捕令表明立场。但他们称这一策略是“对隐私的重大侵犯”，同时强调了不准确数据的潜在可能性。该公司发言人拒绝透露收到的认股权证数量。
谷歌建立了一个特殊的协议来处理这些请求。首先，谷歌搜索Sensorvault，检查哪些用户在指定的时间段内通过该区域，向当局提供匿名信息。在警方决定哪些设备是他们感兴趣的之后，他们可能会迫使谷歌识别特定的用户。
谷歌执法和信息安全主管Richard Salgado在一封电子邮件中表示：“我们在大力保护用户隐私的同时，支持执法部门的重要工作。”
Facebook的一位发言人说，Facebook收到了这样的授权，但没有履行这些授权，因为这家社交媒体平台的位置数据不如设备制造商精确，而且数据的存储方式也存在限制。他拒绝解释这些限制。
拼车公司Lyft的一位女发言人说，如果搜查令不针对“大范围内的所有用户”，或者不针对“过长的时间段”等标准，拼车公司Lyft可能会遵守。她没有定义这些术语。
健身跟踪公司Garmin还没有收到这样的请求，也不会满足它们，因为“这将非常侵犯我们用户的隐私权，”一位代表说。
在冠状病毒大流行期间，在纽约禁止此类搜查的法案已经陷入停滞，但塞普尔韦达和州参议院的另一位共同发起人、参议员约翰·刘(John Liu)希望，随着危机的消退，该法案能再次得到讨论。
Facebook发言人表示，该公司一直“支持”该法案，但拒绝详细说明。谷歌的萨尔加多说，公司很高兴看到关于“需要制定规则来规范执法部门对数据的无目标访问”的讨论。
纽约州参议员塞普尔韦达在一次采访中敦促谷歌加大对当局的反击力度。“如果人们意识到发生了什么，他们将损失惨重，”他说。
note_en:
Police use of a type of warrant to monitor Google users’ locations en masse is staring down its first legal and political challenges as scrutiny of law enforcement tactics grows.
  Criminal defendants in Virginia and San Francisco are disputing “geofence” warrants, which authorities can use to scan geographic areas and time periods for suspects through user location histories stored by technology companies. As these motions await arguments and potential decisions as soon as August, New York lawmakers are pushing legislation to ban the practice.
  Police turned in both cases to  Alphabet  Google, which says the number of such requests grew 1,500% from 2017 to 2018, and an additional 500% from 2018 to 2019. The search and advertising giant created a special process to fulfill the warrants but has raised concerns that the demands made by law enforcement invade privacy.
  Privacy advocates fear that compliance by Google could lead authorities to seek similar data from fitness trackers, ride-share apps and other companies if more of those businesses begin to store users’ granular location histories to hone their products and services.
    “We’re going to see this ever-expanding universe of geolocation warrants that will be tracking huge numbers of people if we don’t outlaw the practice,” said Albert Fox Cahn, executive director of the Surveillance Technology Oversight Project, a nonprofit advocacy group.
  The pushback coincidentally comes amid a drumbeat of criticism of law enforcement after the killing of George Floyd in Minneapolis police custody in May.
  New York state Sen. Luis Sepúlveda, a Democrat who chairs the state senate’s Crime Victims, Crime and Correction Committee, co-sponsored a bill in April to outlaw the warrants and informal requests for such data. Mr. Sepúlveda warned that geofence warrants would be used disproportionately in communities of color like many other police tactics, such as stop-and-frisk.
  San Francisco public defenders filed a motion last month to quash such a warrant used in the February 2019 arrest of LaQuan Dawes, 30, in connection with a home burglary in the city’s Sunset District. They argued authorities’ search for Google users within a half-block radius of the house over more than a two-hour period, which eventually zeroed in on Mr. Dawes, violated his Fourth Amendment rights against unreasonable search and seizure.
  Separately in Virginia, defense lawyers are seeking an August hearing to make similar arguments on behalf of Okello Chatrie, 25, who is accused of armed robbery of a bank outside of Richmond.
  The tech company created a three-step process for fulfilling authorities’ geofence warrants while maintaining as much user privacy as possible, according to court documents.
  Google searches its entire database of accounts with location history enabled to check which users passed through the general area and time period specified in a warrant. The company compiles the information into an anonymized data set for police.
  Authorities review which devices may be of interest, potentially asking for additional location data about specific users for context.
  After police requested information about devices that passed within 150 meters of the crime scene over an hourlong period, Google combed through every user who opted into the location-history feature to narrow down potential matches, according to documents filed in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia.
  Roughly one-third of all Google users opted in as of last year, according to the company’s filings in the case, sending information related to tens or potentially hundreds of millions of people to a database known internally as Sensorvault. A Google representative declined to provide precise numbers.
  Lawyers for the company didn’t take a position on the warrant in Mr. Chatrie’s case, according to court documents. But they called the tactic “a significant incursion on privacy” while highlighting a potential for imprecise data. A company spokesman declined to share the number of warrants it receives.
  Google built a special protocol to process the requests. First, Google searches Sensorvault to check which users passed through the area and during the time period specified, providing anonymized information to authorities. After police decide which devices are of interest, they may compel Google to identify specific users.
  “We vigorously protect the privacy of our users while supporting the important work of law enforcement,” Richard Salgado, Google’s director of law enforcement and information security, said in an email.
    Facebook  receives such warrants but doesn’t fulfill them because the social media platform has less-precise location data than device makers and limitations in how the data is stored, a spokesperson said. He declined to explain those limitations.
  Ride-sharing company  Lyft  potentially would comply if warrants don’t target “all users in a large geographical area,” a spokeswoman said, or for “overly broad time periods,” among other criteria. She didn’t define those terms.
  Fitness-tracking firm  Garmin  hasn’t received such requests and wouldn’t fulfill them because “it would be very invasive of our users’ privacy rights,” according to a representative.
    The bill to ban such searches in New York has stalled amid the coronavirus pandemic, but Mr. Sepúlveda and Sen. John Liu, another co-sponsor in the state senate, hope to take it up again as the crisis subsides.
  The Facebook spokesperson said the company has “been supportive” of the bill but declined to elaborate. Google’s Mr. Salgado said the company is encouraged to see a discussion about “the need for rules to govern untargeted access to data by law enforcement.”
  Mr. Sepúlveda, the New York state senator, urged in an interview that Google push back harder against authorities. “They stand to lose a lot if people realize what’s going on,” he said.
title: 随着警方对谷歌用户位置历史记录的要求越来越多，“地理围栏搜查令”正面临越来越多的抵制和法律审查，在CA和VA面临挑战
title_en: As police requests for Google users' location history grow, “geofence warrants” are facing increasing pushback and legal scrutiny, with challenges in CA and VA

note:
华盛顿--去年9月，亚马逊(Amazon)首席执行官杰夫·贝佐斯(Jeff Bezos)在距离白宫两个街区的著名国家记者俱乐部(National Press Club)上，滔滔不绝地讲述了有关全球变暖的可怕数据。然后他说他有一些激动人心的事情要宣布。
但当他揭开一个带有消息的高耸招牌时，亚马逊的名字却不见踪影。取而代之的是，该标志引入了气候承诺，这是一个减少公司碳排放的项目。是的，亚马逊将是第一个，也是当时唯一的签字国。但贝佐斯说，这是一个更大的推动。
这次活动反映了贝佐斯对美国首都的态度。他抓住机会把自己塑造成一位政治家-“华盛顿邮报”的救世主，在这个国家的精英阶层中占有一席之地。与此同时，他避免了日常琐事，即加强亚马逊对政策制定者的影响力。
但周三贝佐斯首次在国会作证时，情况就不同了。Alphabet、苹果和Facebook的首席执行官将加入他的行列，作为议员们对最大科技公司权力的调查的一部分。预计他将面临猛烈的批评，问题多种多样，如亚马逊的劳动条件、市场力量以及他作为世界首富的地位。
“这不是传统的游说，”美国在线(America Online)联合创始人史蒂夫·凯斯(Steve Case)在谈到他认为是老朋友的贝佐斯到目前为止是如何与华盛顿接触时说。“这更多的是一项长期的关系建设--一点点建立声誉的努力--必须持续数十年。”
他于2013年来到华盛顿，引起了轰动，当时他以2.5亿美元的价格从长期所有者手中收购了《华盛顿邮报》(The Washington Post)，并赋予了该报新的生命。2016年，贝佐斯买下了该市最大的房子，一座2.7万平方英尺的豪宅，曾是卡洛拉马社区的博物馆，前总统贝拉克·奥巴马和其他政治领导人住在那里。
虽然贝佐斯在纽约的影响力有所增加，但亚马逊也是如此，因为它开始向影响政策制定者的传统模式投入大量资金。根据响应性政治中心(Center For Responsive Politics)的数据，2019年，它在联邦游说上花费了1680万美元，高于2015年的不到1000万美元。据其披露，去年，它向智库和协会捐赠了1110万美元，是前一年的两倍多。2018年，它选择了距离华盛顿有地铁车程的弗吉尼亚州水晶城(Crystal City)作为第二个总部所在地。
贝佐斯偶尔现身支持该公司的努力。例如，2017年，他在互联网协会(Internet Association)的年度盛会上接受了互联网协会(Internet Association)负责人的采访。互联网协会是一个代表亚马逊和其他科技巨头的游说团体。
但就像他对亚马逊的许多部门所做的那样，贝佐斯对其政策和公关部门采取了不插手的方式，该部门在全球已发展到800多名员工。他会途经华盛顿参加一年一度的亚马逊董事会会议，全年都会有几次悄悄的访问。
他一直避免与公司最尖锐的批评者举行高调会议，比如Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)几周前与抵制其公司广告的组织者举行的一次会议。贝佐斯没有像谷歌母公司Alphabet的掌门人桑达尔·皮查伊(Sundar Pichai)在2018年那样，养成招待忧心忡忡的议员的习惯。与苹果的蒂姆·库克(Tim Cook)不同，贝佐斯与特朗普总统没有建立起密切的关系。
亚马逊的政治关系工作留给了其他高管。2013年，当奥巴马参观亚马逊的一个仓库时，是该公司的后起之秀戴夫·克拉克(Dave Clark)带他参观的。最近几年，奥巴马的前新闻秘书杰伊·卡尼(Jay Carney)已经成为亚马逊与立法者互动的代言人。
卡尼给纽约州州长安德鲁·M·库默(Andrew M.Cuomo)打了电话，说在遭到当地活动人士和政界人士的强烈反对后，亚马逊放弃了在该州设立第二个总部的承诺。当来自佛蒙特州的进步无党派人士参议员伯尼·桑德斯(Bernie Sanders)推动公司提高最低工资时，他成功地应对了这场危机。
当特朗普还是一名胜算不大的候选人时，贝佐斯在Twitter上发帖称，他想“#发送唐纳德空间”(#Send DonaldtSpace)。但自从特朗普当选以来，贝佐斯一直保持沉默，尽管总统抨击了“华盛顿邮报”，称该报是在按照亚马逊的要求行事，但没有提供证据。该报由贝佐斯私人所有，而不是亚马逊。
“杰夫有点耸耸肩，说这有点合情理，”凯斯说。“我肯定他不喜欢，但他还是接受了。”
到了2018年，“华盛顿人”(Washington Ingtonian)杂志报道说，贝佐斯已经“悄悄地成为了一名随心所欲的华盛顿社交名流”，旁边还有一幅照片插图，插图显示他高耸在华盛顿纪念碑(Washington Monument)之上。华盛顿人寿(Washington Life)对该地区的富裕居民进行了令人屏息的跟踪调查，并将他评为该市最有权势的100人之一。11月，他在史密森学会(Smithsonian Institution)的国家肖像画廊(National Porture Gallery)晚会上获得了一个奖项，该画廊委托他收藏肖像画。
近年来，贝佐斯的知名度也有所提高。去年，他宣布与妻子麦肯齐·贝佐斯(MacKenzie Bezos)离婚，几天后，“国家询问报”(National Enquirer)曝光了他与前电视主持人劳伦·桑切斯(Lauren Sanchez)的婚外情。然后他指责这家小报“敲诈和勒索”，称它威胁要发布猥亵照片，除非他说这家靠近白宫的媒体报道他的恋情没有政治动机。
今年1月，他终于在自己的豪宅中亮相，招待了政界和商界的知名人物。邀请函是从“华盛顿邮报”的一个电子邮件地址发出的，署名很简单，就是“杰夫”。
宾客们混杂在房子的楼下和露台后院，包括伊万卡·特朗普(Ivanka Trump)和她的丈夫贾里德·库什纳(Jared Kushner)等政府人物，摩根大通(JPMorgan Chase)首席执行官杰米·戴蒙(Jamie Dimon)等企业巨头，以及演员本·斯蒂勒(Ben Stiller)等文化名人。
犹他州共和党参议员米特·罗姆尼(Mitt Romney)也在现场。几周后，他在谈到这座房子时说：“它与最初的设计非常一致，而且做得很有品味。”
罗姆尼说，他在派对上只与贝佐斯进行了简短的交谈，以感谢他的盛情款待，但他说，他已经与另一位著名嘉宾比尔·盖茨(Bill Gates)就气候变化和核能问题进行了交谈。
本周国会山的环境可能会变得不那么友好。最近几个月，贝佐斯的财富增加了500多亿美元，而与此同时，大流行期间失业率飙升，使他成为不平等的化身。随着越来越多的美国人因为冠状病毒而被迫在网上购物，对亚马逊主导地位的质疑也变得更加响亮。仓库工作人员表示，亚马逊在追求快速送货的过程中，正将他们置于感染病毒的风险之中。
尽管政界人士的担忧变得更加明显，亚马逊仍拒绝将贝佐斯送到国会。在立法者威胁要传唤他的证词后，该公司同意将他送去。
司法委员会反垄断小组委员会主席、罗德岛州民主党众议员大卫·西西林(David Cicilline)在5月份的一条推文中表示：“没有人可以凌驾于法律之上，无论多么富有或多么有权势。”
凯斯说，议员们不应指望贝佐斯会感到不安。他回忆起两年前贝佐斯与私募股权大亨大卫·鲁宾斯坦(David Rubenstein)一起出现在华盛顿特区经济俱乐部(Economic Club)的舞台上。贝佐斯阐述了各种话题，包括他刚刚宣布的20亿美元基金，用于支持教育和无家可归者。
凯斯在活动中与贝佐斯的父母共用一张桌子，他说，房间里的许多人都不认识贝佐斯，但他们给人留下了深刻的印象。凯斯说，贝佐斯在明确准备好的谈话要点和“不插电的杰夫只是作为杰夫”之间摇摆不定。“他最好的大使就是他自己。”
note_en:
WASHINGTON — Last September, Jeff Bezos, Amazon’s chief executive, rattled off terrifying statistics about the warming planet from the storied National Press Club, two blocks from the White House. Then he said he had something exciting to announce.
 But when he uncovered a towering sign with the news, Amazon’s name was nowhere in sight. Instead, the sign  introduced the Climate Pledge, a project to reduce carbon emissions from companies. Yes, Amazon would be the first, and at the time only, signatory. But this was a bigger push, Mr. Bezos said.
  The event reflected Mr. Bezos’ approach to the nation’s capital. He has jumped at opportunities to cast himself as a statesman — the savior of The Washington Post, who holds court among the country’s elite. At the same time, he has eschewed the day-to-day grind of bolstering Amazon’s influence with policymakers.
 But that changes on Wednesday, when Mr. Bezos testifies before Congress for the first time. He will be joined by the chief executives of Alphabet, Apple and Facebook as part of lawmakers’ investigations into the power of the largest tech companies. He is expected to face an onslaught of critiques, with questions as varied as Amazon’s labor conditions and market power and his status as the richest person in the world.
 “It’s not traditional lobbying,” Steve Case, the America Online co-founder, said of how Mr. Bezos, whom he considers an old friend, had approached Washington until now. “It is much more of a longer-term relationship-building — a little bit of a reputation-building — effort that has to be sustained over decades.”
  He arrived in Washington with a splash in 2013, when he  bought The Washington Post from its longtime owners for $250 million and gave the paper new life. In 2016, Mr. Bezos bought the biggest home in the city, a 27,000-square-foot manse that used to be a museum in the Kalorama neighborhood, where former President Barack Obama and other political leaders live.
  While Mr. Bezos’ presence in the city grew, so did Amazon’s, as it began pouring money into the traditional modes of influencing policymakers. It spent $16.8 million on federal lobbying in 2019, up from less than $10 million in 2015, according to  the Center for Responsive Politics. Last year, it gave $11.1 million to think tanks and associations, more than twice as much as the previous year, according to its  disclosures. In 2018, it selected Crystal City, Va., a Metro ride away from Washington, as the site of its second headquarters.
 Mr. Bezos occasionally appeared in support of the company’s efforts. In 2017, for example, he was interviewed by the head of the Internet Association, a lobbying group that represents Amazon and other tech giants, at its annual gala.
 But as he does with many parts of Amazon, Mr. Bezos took a hands-off approach with its policy and communications group, which has grown to more than 800 employees globally. He’d come through Washington for the annual Amazon board meeting, with a few quiet visits sprinkled throughout the year.
 He has avoided high-profile meetings with his company’s sharpest critics, like the one Mark Zuckerberg, Facebook’s chief executive, held  a few weeks ago with organizers of an ad boycott of his company. Mr. Bezos has not made a habit of glad-handing worried lawmakers, the way Sundar Pichai, who runs Alphabet, Google’s parent company,  did in 2018. And  unlike Tim Cook of Apple, Mr. Bezos has not developed a close relationship with President Trump.
 The work of Amazon’s political relations was left to other executives. In 2013, when Mr. Obama toured an Amazon warehouse, it was Dave Clark, a rising star at the company, who showed him around. In more recent years, Jay Carney, Mr. Obama’s former press secretary, has become the face of Amazon’s interactions with lawmakers.
 Mr. Carney was the one who called Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo of New York to say Amazon was backing out on its commitment to place a second headquarters in the state after facing a backlash from local activists and politicians. And he managed the crisis when Senator Bernie Sanders, the progressive independent from Vermont, pushed the company to raise its minimum wage.
 When Mr. Trump was still a long-shot candidate, Mr. Bezos  tweeted that he wanted to “#sendDonaldtospace.” But since Mr. Trump’s election, Mr. Bezos has remained quiet even as the president attacked The Washington Post, stating, without providing evidence, that the paper was doing Amazon’s bidding. The newspaper is owned privately by Mr. Bezos, not Amazon.
  “Jeff kind of shrugs his shoulders and says it kind of goes with the territory,” Mr. Case said. “I’m sure he doesn’t like it, but he takes it.”
 By 2018, Washingtonian magazine  reported that Mr. Bezos had “quietly become a freewheeling D.C. socialite” alongside a photo illustration that showed him towering over the Washington Monument. Washington Life — which breathlessly tracks the area’s wealthy residents — named him  one of the 100 most powerful people in the city. In November, he received an award at the Smithsonian Institution’s National Portrait Gallery gala, which had commissioned his portrait for its collection.
 Mr. Bezos’ celebrity has also increased in recent years. Last year, he announced that he and his wife,  MacKenzie Bezos, were divorcing, which was followed days later by a National Enquirer exposé of an extramarital affair with Lauren Sanchez, a former television host. Then  he accused the tabloid of “extortion and blackmail,” saying it had threatened to publish lewd photos unless he said the outlet, which is close to the White House, was not politically motivated in reporting on his relationships.
 In January, he finally debuted his mansion, hosting prominent figures in politics and business. The invitations, sent from an email address at The Post, were signed simply “Jeff.”
 Mingling in the home’s downstairs area and terraced backyard, the guests included administration figures like Ivanka Trump and her husband, Jared Kushner; corporate titans like Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JPMorgan Chase; and cultural celebrities like the actor Ben Stiller.
 Senator Mitt Romney, a Republican from Utah, was there as well. “It’s very much consistent with its original design and is tastefully done,” he said of the house a few weeks later.
 Mr. Romney said he had spoken only briefly with Mr. Bezos at the party to thank him for his hospitality but said he had gotten to talk with another notable guest, Bill Gates, about climate change and nuclear power.
 The environment on the Hill this week is likely to be far less hospitable. Mr. Bezos’ wealth has grown by more than $50 billion in recent months, just as unemployment has skyrocketed during the pandemic, making him an avatar for inequality. Questions about Amazon’s dominance have also grown louder, as more Americans have been forced to shop online because of the coronavirus. Warehouse workers have said that Amazon is putting them at risk of contracting the virus in the company’s pursuit of speedy deliveries.
  Even as the concerns of politicians became more pronounced, Amazon resisted sending Mr. Bezos before Congress. The company agreed to send him after lawmakers threatened to subpoena his testimony.
 “No one is above the law, no matter how rich or powerful,”  Representative David Cicilline, the Rhode Island Democrat who leads the Judiciary Committee’s antitrust subcommittee, said in a May tweet.
 Mr. Case said lawmakers should not expect Mr. Bezos to get rattled. He recalled when Mr. Bezos appeared onstage two years ago at the Economic Club of Washington, D.C., with David Rubenstein, a private equity magnate. Mr. Bezos expounded on a variety of topics, including his just-announced $2 billion fund to support education and the homeless.
 Mr. Case, who shared a table at the event with Mr. Bezos’ parents, said that many people in the room did not know Mr. Bezos, but that they had left impressed. Mr. Bezos bounced between clearly prepared talking points and “unplugged Jeff just being Jeff,” Mr. Case said. “His best ambassador is himself.”
title: 杰夫·贝佐斯(Jeff Bezos)对华盛顿采取了不插手的方式，保持在争执之上，但随着他周三首次在国会作证，这种情况发生了变化
title_en: Jeff Bezos has taken a hands-off approach to Washington, staying above the fray, but that changes with his first-ever Congressional testimony on Wednesday

note_en:
SAN FRANCISCO—Google will keep its employees home until at least next July, making the search-engine giant the first major U.S. corporation to formalize such an extended timetable in the face of the coronavirus pandemic.
  The move will affect nearly all of the roughly 200,000 full-time and contract employees across Google parent Alphabet Inc., and is sure to pressure other technology giants that have slated staff to return as soon as January.

title: 在冠状病毒大流行期间，谷歌将把员工留在家里直到2021年夏天
title_en: Google to Keep Employees Home Until Summer 2021 Amid Coronavirus Pandemic
